description.en: Thailand's largest island, famous for beaches, nightlife, water sports and island hopping.
description.nl: Het grootste eiland van Thailand, beroemd om stranden, uitgaan, watersport en eilandhoppen.

enhanced_description:
As the sun dips below the horizon, casting a golden glow over the Andaman Sea, the gentle sound of waves lapping against the shore fills the air. This is Phuket, Thailand's largest island, where the salty breeze carries whispers of adventure and the tantalizing aroma of street food mingles with the scent of tropical flowers. The vibrant colors of the sunsets here are a daily reminder that life is a celebration, and it’s a place where every moment feels alive. 

When I first arrived in Phuket, I was drawn into its rhythm—the bustling markets in Old Town, where the pastel-colored Sino-Portuguese buildings stand as a testament to the island's rich history. I remember wandering through the streets, the chatter of locals blending with the sounds of sizzling woks. One evening, I stumbled upon a night market where an elderly woman was making fresh spring rolls. Her hands moved gracefully as she filled each roll with crunchy vegetables and aromatic herbs. I couldn’t resist and ended up sitting on a small plastic stool, sharing laughter and stories with her as I savored the flavor of her food. It was moments like these that made me realize Phuket is not just about its stunning beaches or vibrant nightlife; it's about the connections you make. 

Unlike other Thai destinations, Phuket offers a unique blend of relaxation and exhilaration. You can spend your day lounging on the soft sands of Kata Beach before heading to Patong for a night of dancing and revelry. But beyond the party scene, there’s a deeper soul to the island. The Big Buddha, standing majestically at 45 meters tall, is not just a tourist attraction; it’s a spiritual haven. I remember climbing up the winding path to reach it, where I was greeted by the serene atmosphere and the sound of monks chanting. The view from the top was breathtaking, a sweeping panorama of the island that made me feel both small and connected to something larger. 

What truly sets Phuket apart is its spirit of adventure. From island hopping to hidden coves, the possibilities are endless. One weekend, I decided to take a longtail boat to Koh Phi Phi. As we sped across the turquoise waters, the thrill of wind in my hair and the sun on my face was intoxicating. Upon arrival, I found myself snorkeling in crystal-clear waters, surrounded by colorful fish darting around me. It was pure bliss, a reminder of why I fell in love with this island in the first place. 

Phuket is a place where every corner has a story, and every experience is an opportunity to dive deeper into its culture. Whether it’s tasting the sweet and spicy flavors of local cuisine or engaging in a traditional Muay Thai class, there is always something new to discover. As I reflect on my time here, I realize that Phuket is not just a destination; it’s a journey of the senses, a tapestry woven from the vibrant threads of nature, culture, and community. It’s a place that beckons you to explore, to connect, and to savor life’s simple pleasures. And trust me, once you’ve experienced it, you’ll find it hard to leave.

hidden_gems.name: Laem Singh Viewpoint
hidden_gems.story: I stumbled upon Laem Singh while looking for a quiet spot away from the crowds. The view took my breath away; it felt like a secret slice of paradise. I met a local artist there who shared his sketches of the view, and we spent the afternoon exchanging stories.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Head north from Kamala Beach, look for a small path leading through the trees. It can be tricky, but the view is worth it.
local_insights: Bring water and snacks; it's a bit of a trek and there's no food nearby.

hidden_gems.name: Koh Sirey
hidden_gems.story: I found Koh Sirey when I decided to take a bike ride around Phuket Town. This small island is often overlooked, but the fishing village there is so charming. I had a delightful chat with a fisherman about his daily catch.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Take the bridge from Phuket Town, and follow the signs to the fishing village.
hidden_gems.best_time: Early morning to see the fishermen at work.
local_insights: Try to learn a few Thai phrases; it goes a long way in connecting with the locals.

hidden_gems.name: Koh Yao Noi
hidden_gems.story: A local friend invited me to his family's home on Koh Yao Noi for a weekend. The island is serene, with stunning beaches and warm hospitality. We spent our days cycling around and enjoying homemade meals.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Catch a ferry from Bang Rong Pier; it’s about a 30-minute ride.
hidden_gems.best_time: Visit during the week to avoid weekend crowds.
local_insights: Bring cash; there are few ATMs on the island.

authentic_experiences.activity: Joining a local Muay Thai training session
authentic_experiences.story: I signed up for a Muay Thai class thinking it would be a fun workout. The trainer was tough but incredibly supportive. By the end of the session, I was sweaty and exhausted, but I felt a sense of accomplishment and camaraderie with the other participants.
authentic_experiences.cultural_significance: Muay Thai is not just a sport; it's a part of Thai culture and history.
authentic_experiences.how_to_participate: Look for local gyms in Patong or Kamala; they often have beginner classes.
insights: Respect the traditions; bow to the ring and the trainers before starting.

authentic_experiences.activity: Participating in a local cooking class
authentic_experiences.story: I took a cooking class in a local home where the chef taught us how to make green curry from scratch. The joy of tasting my own creation was unmatched, and I still use the recipes today.
authentic_experiences.cultural_significance: Cooking is a way to connect with Thai culture and its flavors.
authentic_experiences.how_to_participate: Look for classes offered by local families; they often include market tours.
insights: Ask questions; locals love sharing their culinary secrets.

foodie_adventures.story: I remember the first time I had Pad Thai here; it was nothing like the versions back home. The flavors were vibrant, and the texture was perfect. Watching them prepare it fresh was a spectacle in itself.
ordering_tips: Ask for extra lime and peanuts; it enhances the flavor.

foodie_adventures.story: I was recommended this place by a local friend. The Massaman curry was rich and comforting, served with fluffy rice. It felt like a warm hug on a rainy day.
ordering_tips: Don’t forget to ask for their homemade roti to go with it.

foodie_adventures.story: The first time I tried Som Tum, I was blown away by the freshness and the perfect balance of spicy, sour, and sweet. The chef even taught me how to pick the right papaya.
ordering_tips: Specify your spice level; it can get intense!

local_insights.observation: The kindness of locals is overwhelming.
local_insights.tip: Learn a few Thai phrases; it opens doors.
local_insights.surprise: I was invited to a local wedding after making friends at a market.

local_insights.observation: The nightlife is vibrant but can be overwhelming.
local_insights.tip: Explore quieter bars for a more laid-back vibe.
local_insights.surprise: I found a hidden rooftop bar with amazing views that felt like a secret escape.

best_times.November: The start of the high season, perfect for beach activities.
best_times.February: Ideal time for cultural festivals and outdoor events.

seasonal_foods.rainy season: Fresh seafood is plentiful during this time.
seasonal_foods.dry season: Street food stalls pop up everywhere, offering seasonal fruits.

insider_tips.dry season: Book accommodations in advance; they fill up quickly.
insider_tips.rainy season: Don’t shy away from the rain; it cools the air and makes for cozy evenings.

daily_costs.breakfast: 40 baht for rice porridge (jok) from a street vendor
daily_costs.lunch: 60-80 baht for Thai fried rice or noodles at a local stall
daily_costs.dinner: 100-150 baht for a Thai meal at a local restaurant (seafood near Patong costs much more)
daily_costs.massage: 250-500 baht (walk a few streets from tourist strip for better prices)
daily_costs.smart_bus: 100 baht per ride from airport (day pass 300 baht)
daily_costs.tuk_tuk: 200-400 baht for a short ride near Bangla Road (overpriced!)
daily_costs.nightlife_bangla: ~3,000 baht for an evening on Bangla Walking Street

money_saving_tricks: Take the Smart Bus from the airport (100 THB) instead of a taxi or minibus.
Download and use the Grab app for all transport — much cheaper than tuk-tuks.
Eat Thai food at local restaurants instead of tourist-area seafood places — same quality, fraction of the price.
For fresh affordable seafood, skip Patong and go to fish markets in Kata or Karon.
Book excursions from beach-side tour operators, never through your hotel (they add commission).
Walk 2-3 streets away from Patong Beach for massages at half the price.
Shop at Jungceylon or Central malls instead of street vendors — better prices on real products.
Visit in low season (May-October) for 30-50% lower prices on everything.
where_to_splurge: A day tour to Phi Phi Islands — one of the most beautiful places in Thailand.
A sunset dinner at a rooftop restaurant overlooking the Andaman Sea.
A traditional Thai spa experience at a quality resort spa.
A private longtail boat trip to James Bond Island and Phang Nga Bay.
hidden_costs: Tuk-tuks near Bangla Road charge 200-400 baht for a 3-4 minute ride — use Grab instead.
Airport minibuses make stops at tour offices that pressure you into buying overpriced packages.
Hotels may swap your room for a lower category in high season — always screenshot your booking.
Motorbike fines from tourist police for no helmet or no international license — save yourself the hassle.
National park entrance fees are sometimes not included in tour prices — always confirm beforehand.
Beach activity prices (jet ski, parasailing) at Patong are heavily inflated — always negotiate.

top_attractions.name: Big Buddha (Phra Phuttha Ming Mongkol Ashtamangala)
top_attractions.description: Iconic 45-meter tall white marble Buddha statue overlooking Phuket with panoramic island views. Located on Nakkerd Hill in Chalong.

top_attractions.name: Patong Beach
top_attractions.description: Phuket's most popular beach with clear waters, water sports facilities, and vibrant beach clubs. Center of tourism activity.

top_attractions.name: Phi Phi Islands (Day Trip)
top_attractions.description: Twin limestone islands featuring pristine beaches, crystal-clear snorkeling waters, and dramatic cliffs. Accessible via boat tour.
top_attractions.entrance_fee: 400 THB (12 USD) national park fee per person

top_attractions.name: James Bond Island (Khao Phing Kan)
top_attractions.description: Famous limestone rock formation featured in James Bond film with sea kayaking opportunities through mangrove channels.

top_attractions.name: Phuket Old Town (Sino-Portuguese District)
top_attractions.description: Historic downtown area with colorful colonial architecture, local markets, and traditional Thai-Chinese buildings.

top_attractions.name: Wat Chalong
top_attractions.description: Phuket's most sacred Buddhist temple featuring ornate traditional Thai architecture, golden spires, and spiritual significance.

top_attractions.name: Phuket FantaSea
top_attractions.description: Nightly cultural show featuring Thai mythology performances, elephant shows, and dinner buffet experience.

top_attractions.name: Kata Beach
top_attractions.description: Less crowded alternative to Patong with calm waters perfect for families and swimming, along with restaurants and shops.

top_restaurants.name: Casa Boho Phuket
top_restaurants.description: Contemporary Latin-inspired bar and restaurant with creative cocktails and fusion cuisine in vibrant atmosphere.

top_restaurants.name: Rock Salt
top_restaurants.description: Upscale seafood restaurant featuring Mediterranean influences with fresh daily catch and refined ambiance.

top_restaurants.name: Sizzle Rooftop Restaurant
top_restaurants.description: High-end steakhouse and seafood restaurant with panoramic rooftop views and premium grilled selections.

top_restaurants.name: La Gritta
top_restaurants.description: Italian restaurant specializing in authentic pizza and Italian cuisine with quality imported ingredients.

top_restaurants.name: Kata On Fire Bar And Grill
top_restaurants.description: Casual beachfront grill restaurant serving seafood and quick bites with laid-back atmosphere in Kata Beach area.

top_restaurants.name: Khao Tom Dibuk
top_restaurants.description: Authentic local Thai rice porridge (khao tom) and comfort food establishment where locals eat for genuine flavors.

top_hotels.name: Hotel Clover Patong Phuket
top_hotels.description: Highly-rated 4-star hotel in central Patong offering comfortable mid-range accommodation with modern amenities.

top_hotels.name: Crest Resort & Pool Villas
top_hotels.description: Upscale resort featuring private pool villas and contemporary design with excellent service standards.

top_hotels.name: Phuket Marriott Resort & Spa, Merlin Beach
top_hotels.description: International 4-star resort chain property with beachfront location, full-service spa, and comprehensive amenities.

top_hotels.name: Phuket Orchid Resort & Spa
top_hotels.description: Comfortable 4-star resort offering spa facilities and well-appointed rooms in convenient location.

top_hotels.name: Thavorn Beach Village Resort & Spa
top_hotels.description: Beach-adjacent 4-star resort with spa services, multiple pools, and village-style layout.

travel_tips: Take the Smart Bus from the airport instead of a taxi or minibus — it costs only 100 THB per ride (day pass 300 THB), runs hourly from 8:00 to 23:30, has WiFi, and takes about 1-1.5 hours to Patong.
Avoid airport minibuses — they make many stops and will take you to a tour office where they try to sell you overpriced packages. Pure time and money waste.
If you take a taxi from the airport, always use the public taxi stand and agree on the price beforehand — Phuket taxis don't have meters like Bangkok.
Screenshot your hotel booking confirmation with room type and photos before arrival. In high season, hotels sometimes swap your room for a lower category. Compare on the spot.
Hotel location matters more than the hotel itself. If you want peace and quiet, avoid the Bangla Road / Patong area. Choose Kata, Karon, or Bangtao instead.
Don't book excursions through your hotel — they add commission. Walk along the main beach road and compare prices at street tour operators instead.
Excursion prices vary by season: expect 1,200-1,500 THB in low season and 2,000-3,000 THB in high season. Always ask if national park entrance fees are included.
Download the Grab app before arriving — it's the safest and most transparent way to get around. Prices are fair and fixed, unlike tuk-tuks.
Tuk-tuks near Bangla Road are extremely expensive: 200-400 THB for just a 3-4 minute ride, especially at night. Use Grab instead.
If renting a motorbike (very cheap), always carry your international driving license and wear a helmet. Tourist police actively fine foreigners who break these rules.
Check a rental motorbike thoroughly before taking it — photograph any existing damage to avoid being charged for scratches you didn't make.
For massages, walk a few streets away from Patong Beach and Bangla Road. You'll find much better quality at lower prices (250-500 THB vs 500+ THB in tourist areas).
Thai food is very cheap everywhere, but seafood is overpriced near Patong. For fresh, affordable seafood, take a short trip to Kata or Karon fish markets.
For shopping, skip the street vendors (overpriced even after bargaining) and head to Jungceylon or Central shopping malls for better deals on original items.
Money exchange rates are consistent across Phuket and offices stay open late. Compare 2-3 places and you'll find they're all similar.
Budget around 3,000 THB for a night out on Bangla Walking Street — it's fun chaos but prices add up fast.
Always follow 'No Swimming' signs on beaches — they indicate dangerous currents. Only swim in designated safe areas.
Negotiate prices for beach activities like jet skiing and parasailing at Patong Beach — listed prices are inflated for tourists. Always ask for safety instructions first.
If you have a connecting international flight after a domestic Thai flight, allow minimum 3 hours between flights — domestic flights are frequently delayed.
Learn a few Thai phrases; locals appreciate the effort and it often gets you better prices and service.

budget_info.notes: Budget includes accommodation, food, transportation, and activities

overview: Phuket, Thailand's largest island, stands as a premier Southeast Asian destination offering a captivating blend of pristine beaches, vibrant culture, and world-class entertainment. With a population of approximately 443,000, this southern Thai province has transformed into a cosmopolitan paradise while retaining its authentic charm. From the energy of Patong Beach to the spiritual serenity of the Big Buddha overlooking the island, Phuket delivers unforgettable experiences for every type of traveler—whether seeking adventure through island hopping to the Phi Phi Islands, cultural immersion in the historic Old Town's Sino-Portuguese architecture, or pure relaxation on family-friendly shores.
thingsToDo: Phuket's attractions cater to diverse interests and budgets. Start with iconic experiences like visiting the 45-meter Big Buddha (free entrance, panoramic views), exploring the vibrant Patong Beach with its exceptional water sports facilities and beach clubs (free access), and discovering the limestone cliffs and pristine snorkeling at Phi Phi Islands (400 THB/12 USD per person national park fee). Adventure seekers should not miss James Bond Island with its legendary movie locations and sea kayaking opportunities (300 THB/9 USD tour). For cultural immersion, attend Phuket FantaSea featuring Thai mythology performances and elephant shows (1,800 THB/53 USD including dinner). Explore Phuket Old Town's colorful Sino-Portuguese buildings and local markets, visit the sacred Wat Chalong temple with its traditional architecture, or relax at the less-crowded Kata and Karon beaches perfect for swimming and water sports. Nightlife enthusiasts should experience the famous Bangla Road entertainment hub with its street performances and vibrant atmosphere.
whereToStay: Phuket offers exceptional accommodation across all price points and preferred areas. Budget travelers should consider guesthouses and hostels in Patong, offering dormitory beds and basic private rooms for $15-30 per night near beaches and nightlife. Mid-range options flourish across the island's main beaches, with 3-star hotels in Patong, Kata, and Karon providing comfortable amenities, pool access, and convenient locations for $50-100 nightly. Luxury seekers will find world-class resorts concentrated in Bangtao, Bang Tao Beach, and the quieter northern areas, offering spa facilities, multiple restaurants, and beachfront access for $150-400+ per night. Each beach area has distinct characteristics: Patong offers maximum energy and convenience, Kata appeals to families with calmer waters, Karon provides a quieter atmosphere, and Bangtao attracts luxury resort experiences. Consider your priorities—nightlife proximity, family-friendliness, or tranquility—when selecting your accommodation base.

gettingAround:
From Phuket Airport, the best option for solo travelers or couples is the Smart Bus: 100 THB per ride (day pass 300 THB), running hourly from 8:00 to 23:30 with WiFi and air conditioning. The ride takes 1-1.5 hours to Patong depending on traffic. For families with luggage, take a public taxi from the designated stand — prices are fixed (no meters in Phuket), so always confirm the fare before departing (approximately 400-500 THB to Patong). Avoid minibuses as they make numerous stops and route you through tour offices selling overpriced packages.

Once on the island, the Grab ride-hailing app is by far the best transport option — transparent pricing ($1-8 per ride), safe drivers, and no negotiation needed. Tuk-tuks are iconic but extremely overpriced in tourist areas: expect 200-400 THB for just a 3-4 minute ride near Bangla Road at night. Motorbike taxis cost 100-200 THB for short distances with fixed pricing. For flexibility, motorbike rental is very affordable (150-300 THB per day) but carries risks: you must carry an international driving license and always wear a helmet, as tourist police actively fine foreigners (800+ THB). Before renting, photograph any existing damage to avoid disputes when returning the bike.
bestTimeToVisit: Phuket's tropical climate features distinct wet and dry seasons that significantly impact your experience. The dry season from November through February offers perfect weather with minimal rainfall, comfortable temperatures (24-32°C), and calm seas—this is peak tourist season with higher prices but ideal beach conditions and water sports. March through May represents hot and humid shoulder season, with temperatures climbing to 35°C, making outdoor activities more challenging but hotels offering better rates. The southwest monsoon season from May through October brings heavy rainfall, occasional storms, and rougher seas, though tourism declines considerably and prices drop 30-50%. December through February is particularly ideal, combining perfect weather, festive holiday atmosphere, and year-round beach accessibility. For budget travelers seeking fewer crowds and lower prices while accepting weather unpredictability, September-October offers excellent value. Avoid the wettest months of September-October if water sports or island hopping are priorities.

budget.description: Budget travelers can enjoy Phuket on $20-40/day. Stay in hostels or guesthouses ($12-25/night in Patong or Kamala, dorm beds 250-400 THB). Eat Thai street food and local restaurants ($1-5 per meal — Thai food is very cheap, just avoid tourist-area seafood restaurants). Use the Smart Bus from the airport (100 THB) and Grab for transport ($1-4 per ride). Free attractions include Big Buddha, Phuket Old Town, and all beaches. Massages start at 250 THB ($7) if you walk a few streets away from the tourist strip.

midrange.description: Mid-range travelers enjoy $50-100/day for comfortable experiences. Expect 3-4 star hotels in Kata, Karon, or Bangtao ($80-150/night). Meals at quality Thai and international restaurants ($5-15 per meal), Grab rides across the island ($2-8), and day tours to Phi Phi Islands or James Bond Island ($35-85 per person depending on season — low season 1,200-1,500 THB, high season 2,000-3,000 THB). Always book tours from beach-side operators rather than your hotel to avoid commission markups.

luxury.description: Luxury travelers should budget $150-400+/day. This includes 5-star resorts in Surin, Bangtao, or Cape Panwa ($200-500/night), fine dining and fresh seafood ($30-80 per meal), private tours ($100-200), premium spa treatments ($50-100 per hour), and a night out on Bangla Walking Street (budget ~3,000 THB/$85). Phuket's luxury scene rivals any destination in Southeast Asia.

safetyTips:
Phuket is generally safe for tourists, but knowing the local dynamics helps you avoid problems. On beaches, always respect 'No Swimming' signs — they indicate dangerous rip currents that cause drownings every year. Only swim in areas with lifeguards present. For water activities like jet skiing and parasailing at Patong Beach, always negotiate prices first (listed prices are heavily inflated), demand safety instructions, and check equipment condition before starting.

Traffic safety is crucial: Phuket's roads are among Thailand's most dangerous for tourists. If you rent a motorbike, you MUST carry an international driving license and wear a helmet at all times. Tourist police specifically target foreigners who break these rules, and fines are substantial. Always photograph a rental bike's existing damage before taking it to avoid being charged for scratches you didn't cause.

Be cautious with hotel bookings during high season — rooms sometimes get swapped for lower categories without notice. Screenshot your booking confirmation with room type, photos, and price before arriving, and compare the room in person when you check in.

Avoid airport minibuses that stop at tour offices selling overpriced excursion packages. When booking tours from beach-side operators (recommended over hotel bookings), always confirm what's included — particularly national park entrance fees, which operators sometimes omit.

In Patong's nightlife area around Bangla Road, keep valuables secure and avoid excessive drinking. Money exchange offices across Phuket offer consistent rates and stay open late, so there's no need to carry large amounts of cash.

faq.question: What's the best way to get from Phuket Airport to Patong?
faq.answer: The cheapest option is the Smart Bus at just 100 THB (about $3), running hourly from 8:00 to 23:30 with WiFi and air conditioning. The journey takes 1-1.5 hours depending on traffic. A day pass costs 300 THB for unlimited rides. For families with luggage, take a public taxi from the airport stand — prices are fixed (no meters in Phuket), so always confirm the fare before departing (approximately 400-500 THB to Patong). Avoid minibuses as they make many stops and route you through tour offices that push overpriced packages.

faq.question: How much does it cost to visit Phuket per day?
faq.answer: Budget travelers can manage on $20-40/day staying in hostels or guesthouses ($12-25/night), eating street food ($1-5/meal), and using Smart Bus or Grab. Mid-range travelers spend $50-100/day with 3-4 star hotels and restaurant meals. Luxury travelers budget $150-400+/day with 5-star resorts and fine dining. Thai food is very cheap, but seafood near Patong is overpriced — for affordable fresh seafood, head to Kata or Karon fish markets instead.

faq.question: Are tuk-tuks expensive in Phuket?
faq.answer: Yes, tuk-tuks in Phuket are notoriously overpriced compared to Bangkok. Near Bangla Road, a 3-4 minute ride costs 200-400 THB ($6-12), especially at night. Download the Grab app instead for transparent, fair pricing ($1-8 per ride). Motorbike taxis offer fixed prices of 100-200 THB for short distances.

faq.question: What's the best time to visit Phuket?
faq.answer: The dry season from November to February offers perfect weather (24-32°C), minimal rainfall, and calm seas — ideal for beaches and water sports but expect higher prices. December-February is peak season with excellent conditions. March-May is hot and humid (35°C) but offers lower rates. The monsoon season (May-October) brings rain and rougher seas; prices drop 30-50% but conditions are unpredictable. September-October offers excellent budget value for travelers accepting occasional rainfall.

faq.question: Do I need a rental motorbike or can I get around without one?
faq.answer: You don't need a motorbike. The Grab app provides reliable, transparent pricing ($1-8 per ride) and is the recommended transport method. Smart Bus costs 100 THB and Grab is significantly cheaper and safer than negotiating with tuk-tuk drivers. Motorbike rental requires an international driving license and helmet (police fine foreigners 800+ THB for violations). Grab is always the safer, more convenient choice.

practicalInfo.travelTimeFromAirport: 1-1.5 hours to Patong by Smart Bus (100 THB, runs 8:00-23:30 hourly, includes WiFi) or 45 minutes by public taxi (fixed price 400-500 THB, no meter — confirm fare before departing)
localTransport: Smart Bus from airport (100 THB/ride, day pass 300 THB, WiFi included, runs 8:00-23:30 hourly)
Grab app (recommended — transparent pricing, $1-8 per ride, 24/7 availability)
Tuk-tuk (200-400 THB short rides, very expensive near Bangla Road, no meters)
Motorbike taxi (100-200 THB fixed price for short distances)
Motorbike rental (150-300 THB per day, requires international license + helmet, police checkpoints active)
Public taxi (fixed price from airport stand, always confirm fare before departing)

contentSources.title: 20 Essential Phuket Travel Tips
contentSources.creator: Live Love Thailand
contentSources.description: Local travel tips with real prices and insider knowledge from an experienced Thailand traveler.
